items.correct: The maximum population an environment can sustainably support given available resources.
items.misconception: How much an animal can physically carry.
items.random_wrong: The size a species can reach.
items.why: Students take 'carrying' literally. It's an ECOLOGICAL concept - the ceiling set by food, water, space, etc. Populations may temporarily exceed it but then crash, or oscillate around it.

items.q: Why does renewable energy require energy storage solutions?
items.correct: Solar and wind generation are intermittent - storage smooths out supply and demand.
items.misconception: Renewable energy can never meet demand.
items.random_wrong: Because they generate too much energy.
items.why: Students dismiss renewables as inadequate. The challenge is intermittency, not insufficiency - the Sun sets, wind drops. Storage (batteries, pumped hydro) addresses timing, not total capacity.

items.q: Roughly what proportion of energy passes from one trophic level to the next?
items.why: Students assume energy is conserved across levels. Most energy is lost as heat (respiration), through movement, indigestible parts, and uneaten material. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short.

items.misconception: Famine is inevitable as numbers grow
items.random_wrong: Birth rates always exceed death rates
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Population pressure drives agricultural innovation. “Famine is inevitable as numbers grow” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Sen's entitlement theory states famines result from:
items.correct: Failure of entitlements, not absolute shortage
items.why: Correct answer: Failure of entitlements, not absolute shortage. “Crop failures alone” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If a country's grain supply is 200 kg per capita yr⁻¹ and the average diet needs 250 kg yr⁻¹, the per-capita deficit (kg yr⁻¹) is?
items.why: Exact answer: 50. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
